coredata.dc:title: Molybdenum disulfide quantum dots for rapid fluorescence detection of glutathione and ascorbic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Linlong
Shao, Kayi
Zhong, Yaping
Guo, Lijuan
Ge, Junping
Lu, Zhentan
Wang, Dong
coredata.dc:description: A method for the specific detection of glutathione (GSH) and ascorbic acid (AA) using 3-aminophenylboronic acid functionalized molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (APBA-MoS2 QDs) was reported. The APBA-MoS2 QDs synthesized using the hydrothermal method showed the strongest fluorescence intensity at an emission wavelength of 375 nm with the optimum excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The detection method was constructed by the combination of the dynamic and static quenching and the inner filter effect (IFE) of APBA-MoS2 QDs by permanganate (MnO4 −) and the rapid redox reaction (1 min) of MnO4 − by GSH or AA. The concentrations of GSH and AA showed good linear ranges of 10–500 μM and 10–100 μM, respectively, with limits of detection (LOD) of 0.476 μM and 0.185 μM, respectively. The spiked recovery tests in human serum and fresh fruit samples showed that APBA-MoS2 QDs had significant potential in the construction of fluorescent biological detection methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molybdenum disulfide quantum dots
Dynamic and static quenching
Inner filter effect
Glutathione detection
Ascorbic acid detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: APBA-MoS2 QDs fluorescence detection of GSH and AA
GSH and AA analysis in real samples
Selective and interference study of APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 − system
Detection mechanism of GSH and AA by APBA-MoS2 QDs
Detection of GSH and AA using APBA-MoS2 QDs as fluorescent probe
APBA-MoS2 QDs assay for the detection of GSH and AA in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful for the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province ( 2023AFB244 ), Young and Middle-aged Talents Project of Hubei Provincial Department of Education ( Q20221712 ), Wuhan Textile University National Project Cultivation Program, and the Opening project of Key Laboratory of Textile Fiber and Products, Ministry of Education ( Fzxw2021023 ), the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2022YFB3805803 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U20A20257 ), the Special Fund of Taishan Industry Leading Talents Project . We also thank \u201CHubei International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Intelligent Textile Materials & Application\u201D and \u201CWuhan Engineering Technology Research Center for Advanced Fibers\u201D for providing partial support for materials processing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of APBA-MoS2 QDs (the inset shows HRTEM image with crystal structure), (b) high-resolution XPS spectra of S 2p, (c) high-resolution XPS spectra of Mo 3d, (d) high-resolution XPS spectra of B 1s, (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs, MoS2 QDs and APBA, and (f) fluorescence spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs (λex = 320 nm; λem = 375 nm).
(a) Different pH values (1–12), (b) different NaCl concentrations (0–800 mM), (c) 2 h UV light irradiation, and (d) different storage times (0–30 d) to study the stability of APBA-MoS2 QDs.

(a) Stern-Volmer (S-V) curves of APBA-MoS2 QDs with different concentrations of MnO4 −; (b) corrected Stern-Volmer (S-V) curve; (c) FTIR spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs and APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 −; (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs, MnO4 −, and APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 −; (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of MnO4 − and the fluorescence spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs; and (f) fluorescence emission spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs, APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 −, APBA-MoS2 QDs/AA, APBA-MoS2 QDs/GSH, APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 −/AA, and APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 −/GSH.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs and MnO4 − (400 μM) mixed with various concentrations of the GSH (0–3000 μM), (b) linear relationship between the GSH concentration and fluorescence intensity, (c) fluorescence spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs and MnO4 − (400 μM) mixed with various concentrations of the AA (0–260 μM), (d) linear relationship between the AA concentration and fluorescence intensity, (e) linear fitting curve of the GSH concentration (10–500 μM) to fluorescence intensity, and (f) linear fitting curve of the AA concentration (10–100 μM) to fluorescence intensity.

(a) Selectivity of APBA-MoS2 QDs to GSH and AA (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni+, Co2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cl−, Br−, and NO2 −) instead of GSH and AA, (b) Selectivity of APBA-MoS2 QDs to GSH and AA (HCO3 −, CO3 2−, H2PO4 2−, HPO4 2−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, S2O3 2−, alanine, glutamate, lysine, phenylalanine, L-cysteine, tyrosine, citric acid, and urea) instead of GSH and AA.
Synthesis of APBA-MoS2 QDs and the Fluorescence Detection Mechanism of GSH and AA.
APBA-MoS2 QDs Fluorescence Detection of GSH in Real Samples (n = 3).
APBA-MoS2 QDs Fluorescence Detection of AA in Real Samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Molybdenum disulfide quantum dots for rapid fluorescence detection of glutathione and ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Textile Fiber and Products (Wuhan Textile University), Ministry of Education, Hubei International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Intelligent Textile Materials &Application, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Textile Fiber and Products (Wuhan Textile University)
Hubei International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Intelligent Textile Materials &Application

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Textile Fiber and Products (Wuhan Textile University), Ministry of Education, Hubei International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Intelligent Textile Materials &Application, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Linlong Zhang and Kayi Shao contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A method for the specific detection of glutathione (GSH) and ascorbic acid (AA) using 3-aminophenylboronic acid functionalized molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (APBA-MoS2 QDs) was reported. The APBA-MoS2 QDs synthesized using the hydrothermal method showed the strongest fluorescence intensity at an emission wavelength of 375 nm with the optimum excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The detection method was constructed by the combination of the dynamic and static quenching and the inner filter effect (IFE) of APBA-MoS2 QDs by permanganate (MnO4 −) and the rapid redox reaction (1 min) of MnO4 − by GSH or AA. The concentrations of GSH and AA showed good linear ranges of 10–500 μM and 10–100 μM, respectively, with limits of detection (LOD) of 0.476 μM and 0.185 μM, respectively. The spiked recovery tests in human serum and fresh fruit samples showed that APBA-MoS2 QDs had significant potential in the construction of fluorescent biological detection methods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) and ascorbic acid (AA) widely present in various organs of the human body as bioactive molecules, and play a direct or indirect role in many important physiological activities [1]. GSH is a tripeptide composed of glycine, cysteine, and glutamic acid, with sulfhydryl groups [2]. As an antioxidant, GSH can participate in the tricarboxylic acid cycle and glucose metabolism in the body, and has the functions of detoxification, delaying aging, preventing diabetes and cancer, and eliminating fatigue [3]. Similar to GSH, AA is a natural antioxidant that serves as a significant neuromodulator in the central nervous system [4,5]. AA is usually ingested into the body from outside sources and is mainly found in fresh fruits and vegetables [6]. AA is essential for human health, with antioxidant, anti-allergic, immune-boosting, and bone-growth-promoting properties [7,8]. Therefore, the detection of AA and GSH is crucial.

Currently, numerous conventional detection methods for GSH and AA have been reported, including electrochemical methods, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), titrimetric methods, spectrophotometric methods, and others [9–13]. However, these methods still have some drawbacks, such as the complexity of the operation process, time-consuming, and susceptibility to interference from the external environment, thus limiting the real-time and efficient detection of GSH and AA. The high sensitivity, stability, ease of operation, and rapid response of fluorescent probe technology have captured the interest of researchers when compared to conventional detection methods [14,15]. For instance, Khezri et al. achieved highly sensitive detection of uric acid in urine samples using fluorescent probe technology [16]. Zarghampour et al. designed a silver nanoprism that can be used for the determination of phenytoin in exhaled breath condensate using fluorescence “off-on” sensing strategy [17]. Fluorescent probes can be divided into organic fluorescent probes, metal ion fluorescent probes, protein-based fluorescent probes, and inorganic and nano-probes, etc [6,18,19]. Compared with other fluorescent probes, quantum dots, as a novel type of nano-materials, have attracted much attention as fluorescent probes on account of their superiorities of good biocompatibility, strong fluorescence, simple preparation process, and low toxicity [20,21]. For instance, Wang et al. developed a ZnS-based quantum dot for GSH detection using an off-off fluorescence sensor [22]. Nandhini Arumugam et al. developed a method for detecting AA in aqueous solutions using a carboxyl-functionalized CdSe-ZnS quantum dot immobilized on graphene oxide [23]. Norah Alhokbany et al. employed carbon quantum dots that were synthesized using a hydrothermal method involving sodium citrate and NH4HCO3 in order to detect AA [24]. However, most of the reported materials can detect only one of AA or GSH, so designing fluorescent quantum dots that can detect both AA and GSH holds tremendous significance.

Nowadays, two-dimensional materials quantum dots are attracting interest. Two-dimensional materials quantum dots are nanoscale materials with unique physical and chemical properties, which usually consist of a single layer or several layers of atoms and have excellent electrical and optical properties [25,26]. Among them, molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (MoS2 QDs) are extensively utilized in fluorescence sensing. This is attributed to their exceptional optical properties, biocompatibility, photostability, and water dispersibility [27]. For instance, Wang et al. used the fluorescence properties of MoS2 QDs to detect curcumin in food samples through the FRET mechanism [28]. Guo et al. designed a boric acid functionalized molybdenum disulfide quantum dot (APBA-MoS2 QD) for ultrasensitive analysis of dopamine [29]. In our previous studies, we also used fluorescent MoS2 QDs to detect doxycycline [30], hypochlorite [31,32] and ascorbic acid [33]. In summary, the development of a method that enables the simultaneous detection of GSH and AA using MoS2 QDs deserves attention.

Previously, we used 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) functionalized MoS2 QDs (hydrothermally synthesized from sodium molybdate and glutathione) to detect hypochlorite by using the quenching effect of 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to QDs and the redox reaction between TMB and hypochlorite [32]. Inspired by this, we proposed a method for the detection of GSH and AA using another APBA functionalized MoS2 QDs with different optical properties (APBA-MoS2 QDs, MoS2 QDs were hydrothermally synthesized from sodium molybdate and L-cysteine). Permanganate (MnO4 −) as a quencher can quench the fluorescence of APBA-MoS2 QDs through the mechanism of dynamic and static quenching and inner filter effect (IFE), GSH and AA can rapidly reduce MnO4 −, thus lowering the concentration of MnO4 − and restoring the fluorescence of APBA-MoS2 QDs (Scheme 1 ). The detection of GSH and AA can be achieved within 1 min, and the detection limits (LOD) are 0.476 μM and 0.185 μM, respectively. The method achieved specific and sensitive detection of AA in fresh fruits and vegetables as well as GSH in human serum. Therefore, the proposed APBA-MoS2 QDs are expected to be used for the detection of GSH and AA.

Sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC·HCl), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and glutathione (GSH) were bought from Aladdin ltd, Shanghai. Phenylalanine, Lysine, Tyrosine, L-Cysteine, Glutamate, Alanine and Citric acid were obtained from MACKLIN reagent, Shanghai. NaH2PO4·2H2O, HCl (37 %), NaCl, NaHCO3, KCl, CaCl2, CuCl2·2H2O, MgCl2·2H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, FeCl3, CoCl2·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, Na2SO4, Na2SO3, Na2S2O3, Na2HPO4·12H2O, Na2CO3, KBr, FeCl2·4H2O, NaNO2, ZnSO4·7H2O, KMnO4, Urea and L-Ascorbic acid (AA) were purchased from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. All the chemical reagents were of analytical grade and were used as received without any other treatment. All solutions were prepared with Milli-Q water.

Transmission electronic microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha), Fluorescence spectrometer (LENGGUANG F97Pro), UV–vis spectroscopy (SHIMADZU UV-2700), Infrared spectrometer (BRUKER VERTEX 70), Dynamic light scattering instrument (DLS, Malvern ZEN 3600).

We used hydrothermal method to synthesize MoS2 QDs, and functionalized them with APBA to synthesize APBA-MoS2 QDs [29]. 0.6 g Na2MoO4·2H2O was dissolved in 60 mL Milli-Q water, and the pH of the solution was modified to 6.5 using 0.1 M HCl after complete dissolution. 1.25 g Cys was dissolved in 100 mL of Milli-Q water. Following this, the Cys solution was combined with the Na2MoO4·2H2O solution in a volume ratio of 1:2 and allowed to react for 10 min. The reaction mixture was then transferred into a high-pressure reactor and maintained at a temperature of 200 °C for a period of 12 h. Once the reaction reached completion, the resulting solution underwent centrifugation at a speed of 8000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 μm filter membrane. After that, the filtrate was collected to obtain the solution of MoS2 QDs with carboxyl and amino functional groups on the surface. The obtained MoS2 QDs solution was blended in a volume ratio of 1:1 into a PBS with a pH of 7.4, after that 1.44 g EDC·HCl and 2.88 g NHS were added, stirred for 12 h, and then 1.08 g APBA was added. Stir in the dark at room temperature for 12 h, and then filter using a 0.45 μm microporous filter membrane. The resulting filtrate was then subjected to dialysis with Milli-Q water for 24 h to obtain APBA-MoS2 QDs, which were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: APBA-MoS2 QDs fluorescence detection of GSH and AA

ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we discussed the optimal concentration of MnO4 −. 0.1 M MnO4 − was prepared and added to 400 μL APBA-MoS2 QDs solution in different volumes, and Milli-Q water was added to make the final volume of the solution to be 2 mL. The changes of fluorescence intensity after adding different concentrations of MnO4 − were recorded to find the best concentration of MnO4 −.

Then, we studied the response time of APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 − system to GSH and AA. 0.1 M GSH solution and AA solution were prepared respectively. 10 μL GSH solution or 5 μL AA solution were added to APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 −, and the final volume was adjusted to 2 mL with Milli-Q water. The changes of fluorescence intensity of the system were recorded at different response times.

The fluorescence detection performance of APBA-MoS2 QDs for GSH and AA were evaluated. To obtain standard solutions with diverse concentrations, GSH and AA were dissolved in Milli-Q water. 400 μL APBA-MoS2 QDs solution and 8 μL MnO4 − solution (0.1 M) were added to 1.5 mL Milli-Q water. Subsequently, the GSH and AA solutions of various concentrations were added individually, and mixed well to make the final volume of the solutions as 2 mL. The fluorescence signal was measured at the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths, the standard curve equation was obtained and the LOD was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GSH and AA analysis in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: We employed APBA-MoS2 QDs to successfully detect GSH levels in human serum (obtained from Hubei University school hospital), as well as AA levels in various fresh fruits and vegetables, including orange, lemon, pear, watermelon, strawberry, tomato, and kiwifruit (purchased from the Wuhan Textile University student supermarket). To obtain fruit juice samples, seven different types of fresh fruits were pressed and filtered using a 0.45 μm filter membrane. These samples were then diluted five times. Afterwards, various concentrations of standard AA solutions were mixed with the fruit juices to create actual samples. The concentrations of AA in these seven samples were subsequently determined. We obtained four different human serums. After filtering them using a 0.45 μm filter membrane, we mixed them with various concentrations of a standard GSH solution to create the actual samples. Finally, we determined the concentrations of GSH in each of the four samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and interference study of APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 − system
ce:section.ce:para: We explored the selectivity of APBA-MoS2 QDs to GSH and AA. We used some reductive substance (Fe2+, SO3 2−, Br−, NO2 −, citric acid, and urea), and some amino acids (alanine, glutamate, lysine, phenylalanine, L-cysteine, and tyrosine), and some common ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni+, Co2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cl−, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, H2PO4 2−, HPO4 2−, SO4 2−, and S2O3 2−) with a 10-fold concentration of GSH and AA to replace GSH and AA for fluorescence detection, and compared their fluorescence signals.

In addition, we also studied the anti-interference ability of APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 − in detecting GSH and AA. We added the same concentration of interfering substances as GSH or AA to the APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 − system, recorded its fluorescence intensity, and then added GSH or AA to observe the change of fluorescence intensity.

We used transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to characterize the morphology of the prepared APBA-MoS2 QDs. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the APBA-MoS2 QDs exhibited a spherical shape and displayed monodispersity when dispersed in water. The lattice parameter of these APBA-MoS2 QDs is measured to be 0.24 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to investigate the composition and surface functional groups of the APBA-MoS2 QDs. Fig. S1a illustrates the XPS spectrum obtained from the APBA-MoS2 QDs, which indicates that APBA-MoS2 QDs are primarily consists of C, N, O, B, S and Mo. The following are the high-resolution XPS spectra of the three primary elements. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 1b), two obvious peaks at 161.8 eV and 163.7 eV correspond to the characteristic peaks of S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 orbitals, respectively. In addition, two peaks at 168.1 eV and 169.5 eV belong to the characteristic peaks of S2O3 2− [34]. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Mo 3d (Fig. 1c), two main characteristic peaks at 232.0 eV and 227.4 eV correspond to Mo(IV) 3d3/2 and Mo(IV) 3d5/2, respectively [35,36]. The peak at 225.5 eV is the characteristic peak of S 2s. The IV oxidation state is dominated by the element Mo present in APBA-MoS2 QDs. Fig. 1d illustrates the high-resolution XPS spectra of B 1s, displaying two prominent peaks at 191.3 eV and 190.7 eV. These peaks correspond to the BO bond and the BC bond, respectively [37]. This indicates the successful surface modification of the quantum dot with the boric acid functional group.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was used to analyze the chemical state of the APBA-MoS2 QDs and MoS2 QDs surface (Fig. S1b). The peak of FTIR spectrum of MoS2 QDs at 1569 cm−1 is the in-plane bending vibration absorption peak of NH bond, the peak at 1404 cm−1 is the stretching vibration absorption peak of CN bond, and the broad peak at 3500–3000 cm−1 is the stretching vibration absorption peak of OH/NH bond. In the FTIR spectrum of APBA-MoS2 QDs, the peak at 1651 cm−1 is the stretching vibration absorption peak of NHCO bond, which is due to the modification of APBA to the surface of MoS2 QDs to form an amide bond. The stretching vibration absorption peak of the CC bond on the benzene is at 1568 cm−1. The peaks at 1390, 1170, 1085 and 1011 cm−1 are ascribed to BO bond stretching vibration, BOH bond bending vibration, CB bond stretching vibration and BOH bond deformation vibration, respectively [29]. This indicates that APBA was successfully grafted onto the surface of MoS2 QDs. UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and FL spectroscopy can be employed to investigate the optical properties of APBA-MoS2 QDs. As displayed in Fig. 1e, APBA-MoS2 QDs have two obvious UV absorption peaks at wavelengths of 237 and 261 nm. The characteristic peak of APBA is responsible for the observed peak at 237 nm, the broad peak at 261 nm is likely a result of the n-π* transition of the NHCO bond [38–40], while MoS2 QDs have no obvious absorption peak near 237 nm. The successful preparation of APBA-MoS2 QDs is evident from the XPS, FTIR and UV–vis absorption results presented above. At the same time, according to the absorbance data of APBA-MoS2 QDs in Fig. 1e, the Tauc curve (Fig. S2) can be drawn by Tauc equation ((αhυ)2 = αo (hυ-Eg)) [41,42], and the Eg (band gap energy) can be calculated to be 5.61 eV [43]. Fig. S3 demonstrates that the emission peak of APBA-MoS2 QDs undergoes a slight red-shift as the excitation wavelength increases within the range of 285–340 nm, which indicates that APBA-MoS2 QDs have excitation-dependent fluorescence emission characteristic. This characteristic contributes to the size effect, surface state and quantum confinement effect of QDs [44,45]. Fig. S3b shows the normalized excitation spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs. From Fig. 1f, the optimal excitation wavelength of APBA-MoS2 QDs is determined to be 320 nm, while the optimal emission wavelength is found to be 375 nm.

When APBA-MoS2 QDs are used as fluorescent probes to detect substances in real samples, they usually need to be suitable to a variety of conditions, so the stability of the newly prepared materials becomes one of the concerns. We studied how the fluorescence properties of APBA-MoS2 QDs varied in response to different pH conditions, ionic strengths, UV irradiation times, and long storage times. The fluorescence intensity of APBA-MoS2 QDs was observed to be higher in neutral and weakly alkaline conditions, as illustrated in Fig. 2 a. Conversely, the fluorescence intensity showed a relative decrease when the QDs were exposed to acidic and strongly alkaline environments. This can be attributed to the presence of hydroxyl and amino functional groups on their surface. These functional groups are not stable under acidic and strong alkaline conditions, respectively, resulting in a low fluorescence intensity. Additionally, we investigated the stability of APBA-MoS2 QDs in various concentrations of NaCl solution. As depicted in Fig. 2b, we observed that the fluorescence intensity of APBA-MoS2 QDs remained relatively constant with increasing salt concentration. Upon reaching a salt concentration of 800 mM, the fluorescence intensity can still be maintained at more than 95 % of the initial fluorescence intensity, which indicates that APBA-MoS2 QDs have good salt resistance and are candidate materials for biological applications. In addition, we irradiated APBA-MoS2 QDs under an ultraviolet lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm for 2 h, and found that the fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged (Fig. 2c), indicating that APBA-MoS2 QDs have good ultraviolet light stability. Finally, we conducted a stability test on APBA-MoS2 QDs by storing them in a refrigerator at 4 °C. Fig. 2d illustrates a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity of APBA-MoS2 QDs over a period of 30 days, but still remained above 80 % of the initial fluorescence intensity, indicating that APBA-MoS2 QDs have good stability. We also studied the stability of APBA-MoS2 QDs in aqueous medium. Fig. S4a shows that the hydrodynamic diameter (HDD) of APBA-MoS2 QDs hardly changed in 10 days, and Fig. S4b demonstrates that the potential value of APBA-MoS2 QDs has a slight change within 10 days, but the change remains within 80 % of the initial value. This indicates that APBA-MoS2 QDs can be stably dispersed in aqueous medium and can be used for long-term storage applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of GSH and AA by APBA-MoS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: When studying the detection mechanism of GSH and AA, we first selected substances that can significantly reduce the fluorescence intensity of APBA-MoS2 QDs. Fig. S5 demonstrates that when various substances of equal concentration were introduced to the APBA-MoS2 QDs solution, it was observed that the fluorescence of APBA-MoS2 QDs only exhibited a substantial decrease upon the addition of MnO4 −. Therefore, MnO4 − was selected as the quencher for the detection of GSH and AA by APBA-MoS2 QDs.

The quenching mechanism of quantum dots includes static quenching, dynamic quenching, energy transfer, photoinduced electron transfer and inner filter effect (IFE), et al [46,47]. Fig. S6 illustrates the Zeta potential measurement results of APBA-MoS2 QDs, MnO4 − and APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 −, this shows that the potentials of the three substances are negative and similar, so there will be electrostatic repulsion, indicating that there is no charge transfer between them. To investigate the quenching mechanism of MnO4 − on APBA-MoS2 QDs, we conducted fluorescence intensity tests on APBA-MoS2 QDs at varying concentrations of MnO4 −. The results were used to generate the Stern-Volmer (S-V) curve, which is depicted in Fig. 3 . The figure can be described by the S-V equation (F0/F1 = 1 + KSV[Q]) [48]. In the equation, F0 represents the fluorescence intensity when there is no quenching by MnO4 −, F1 represents the fluorescence intensity in the presence of MnO4 −, KSV is the quenching constant derived from the S-V equation, and [Q] represents the concentration of the quencher MnO4 −. Fig. 3a clearly demonstrates that the S-V curve exhibits a non-linear behavior characterized by upward curvature. Based on the research findings, the presence of a nonlinear upward curvature in the S-V curve suggests that the quenching mechanism is a combination of dynamic quenching and static quenching [49,50]. Therefore, we modified the S-V equation to obtain Kapp = [(F0/F1) − 1]/[Q] = (KD + KS) + KDKS[Q], where KD and KS represent the constants for dynamic and static quenching, respectively [51]. According to the equation, the curve is drawn as shown in Fig. 3b. The corrected curve still shows a nonlinear upward curvature, which indicates that APBA-MoS2 QDs form a ground state complex with MnO4 − and a small number of fluorophores are quenched by the quencher. It can be concluded that there is static quenching in the quenching mechanism. According to Fig. S7, the characteristic absorption peak of APBA-MoS2 QDs at 237 nm weakened gradually as the concentration of MnO4 − increased. Simultaneously, there was an increase in the absorption peak at 261 nm. This indicates that there is an interaction between MnO4 − and APBA-MoS2 QDs. The FTIR spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs and APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 − (Fig. 3c) showed that the peak at 3366 cm−1 (OH bond) was significantly enhanced after adding different concentrations of MnO4 −, indicating the increase of oxygen-containing functional groups [52]. It was further proved that there was an interaction between APBA-MoS2 QDs and MnO4 −. Combined with the UV–vis spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs, MnO4 −, and APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 − (Fig. 3d), the absorption spectra changed after the addition of MnO4 −, further indicating the presence of static quenching. In addition, Fig. 3e demonstrates that the peak at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm for APBA-MoS2 QDs has a large part of the overlap with the absorption peak of MnO4 − at 310 nm. Thus, the IFE is also one of the reasons leading to the quenching of APBA-MoS2 QDs by MnO4 −. Fig. S8 shows that the excitation peak of APBA-MoS2 QDs at 320 nm is closer to the maximum absorption peak of MnO4 − at 311 nm than the excitation peak of MoS2 QDs at 367 nm, indicating that the introduction of APBA is more favorable to IFE, and thus the fluorescence of QDs can be quenched to a greater degree. In summary, the quenching of APBA-MoS2 QDs by MnO4 − is determined by the dynamic and static quenching and IFE.

Then, we investigated the feasibility of detecting GSH and AA. Fig. 3f reveals that the introduction of MnO4 − into the APBA-MoS2 QDs solution resulted in a notable reduction in fluorescence intensity at the optimal emission wavelength. This can be attributed to the combined effects of dynamic and static quenching as well as IFE. However, the fluorescence intensity experienced a subsequent enhancement upon the successive addition of GSH and AA. When either GSH or AA was added to the APBA-MoS2 QDs solution individually, the fluorescence intensity remained unchanged. This indicates that it is feasible to use MnO4 − as an intermediate substance to achieve the detection of GSH and AA. GSH and AA are natural antioxidants with good reducibility, MnO4 − is a strong oxidant. When GSH or AA is added to APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 −, MnO4 − will undergo redox reaction with GSH and AA, so that the amount of MnO4 − as a quencher is reduced, thereby restoring the fluorescence of APBA-MoS2 QDs. Based on the quenching effect of MnO4 − on APBA-MoS2 QDs and the redox reaction between GSH/AA and MnO4 −, a specific fluorescence sensing technology can be developed using APBA-MoS2 QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH and AA using APBA-MoS2 QDs as fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we investigated the impact of various concentrations of MnO4 − on the quenching of APBA-MoS2 QDs to prevent quencher overload. As illustrated in Fig. S9, the fluorescence intensity of APBA-MoS2 QDs progressively declined as the concentration of the MnO4 − solution increased. However, once the MnO4 − concentration surpassed 400 μM, the fluorescence intensity remained relatively stable. Hence, a concentration of 400 μM was chosen as the optimal quencher concentration. Then, we investigated the detection time for GSH and AA. The change of fluorescence intensity in 0–5 min after the addition of substrate GSH or AA was shown in Fig. S10. Within the first minute, we observed a rapid increase in fluorescence intensity, which then remained stable thereafter. It can be concluded that the detection of GSH and AA by APBA-MoS2 QDs can be completed within 1 min, which indicates that this technique holds great potential for rapid detection applications.

We next investigated the fluorescence sensing properties of GSH and AA. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, GSH with a concentration of 0–3000 μM was added to APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 −, respectively. With an increase in the concentration of GSH, there was a corresponding gradual increase in the fluorescence intensity at a wavelength of 375 nm. Two good linear relationships (Fig. 4e) were obtained by linear fitting with GSH concentration as abscissa and fluorescence intensity as ordinate. The linear equations were Y = 2.97X + 364.27 (R2 = 0.999, 10–80 μM) and Y = 1.35X + 495.11 (R2 = 0.996, 80–500 μM). According to the calculation formula of LOD (LOD = 3σ/m), the LOD of GSH can be calculated to be 0.476 μM. In Fig. 4c and d, as the concentration of AA increased (0–260 μM), there was a corresponding increase in the fluorescence intensity of the solution. We performed a linear fitting with the concentration of AA as the independent variable and the fluorescence intensity as the dependent variable. The results revealed a strong linear relationship (Fig. 4f) described by the equation Y = 7.64X + 407.69 (R2 = 0.994, 10–100 μM). We calculated the LOD for AA to be 0.185 μM.

Table S1 presents the methods reported for detecting GSH and AA. Our method stands out with its short detection time (1 min) and wider linear range (10–500 μM and 10–100 μM) compared to these methods, but our method cannot distinguish the detection of GSH and AA when both of them are present in the detection system. This limitation provides ideas for our future research work.

In order to study whether APBA-MoS2 QDs have specific selection for GSH and AA, we used some reductive substance (Fe2+, SO3 2−, Br−, NO2 −, citric acid, and urea), some amino acids (alanine, glutamate, lysine, phenylalanine, L-cysteine, and tyrosine), and some common ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni+, Co2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cl−, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, H2PO4 2−, HPO4 2−, SO4 2−, and S2O3 2−) with a 10-fold concentration of GSH and AA to replace GSH and AA for fluorescence detection. Fig. 5 illustrates that the fluorescence of the APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 − system can only be significantly restored by the addition of GSH and AA. This observation suggests that the fluorescence detection method utilizing APBA-MoS2 QDs exhibits a high level of selectivity towards GSH and AA.

In addition, we also studied the anti-interference ability of APBA-MoS2 QDs to detect GSH and AA. Fig. S11 is the detection ability of APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 − system to GSH and AA after adding various interfering substances with the same concentration as GSH and AA. It can be seen from the Fig. S11 that the fluorescence intensity is almost unchanged when different interfering substances are added to the APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 − system, and the fluorescence intensity is significantly enhanced when GSH or AA is added to it. This indicates that the presence of interfering substances does not affect the detection of GSH and AA by APBA-MoS2 QDs, which proves that the detection method has good anti-interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: APBA-MoS2 QDs assay for the detection of GSH and AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The APBA-MoS2 QDs have been demonstrated to be a viable fluorescent probe for detecting GSH and AA in real samples. Four different human serum (obtained from Hubei University School Hospital) as well as seven different fresh fruits (purchased from the Wuhan Textile University student supermarket) were selected for the study. After pressing, filtering and diluting, the actual samples were obtained by mixing standard AA solutions of various concentrations with the APBA-MoS2 QDs. Filtered human serum was supplemented with various concentrations of GSH standard solutions. Afterwards, GSH content in human serum and AA content in fresh fruits were analyzed using the assay we worked out, and Table 1 and Table 2 display the obtained results. The initial content of the substrate in each sample, the spiked results of GSH in four human serums (Table 1) and AA in seven fresh fruits (Table 2) were detected. The recovery rate was 97–105 %, and the RSD was less than 7 %. These findings indicate the reliability and accuracy of the fluorescence detection method for detecting GSH and AA.

In this study, a method basessd on APBA-MoS2 QDs to specifically detect GSH and AA is proposed. The dynamic and static quenching of APBA-MoS2 QDs by the quencher MnO4 − and the IFE were utilized to quench the fluorescence of APBA-MoS2 QDs as well as to restore the fluorescence of GSH or AA by redox reaction between GSH or AA and MnO4 −. On account of this, a rapid detection of GSH and AA was achieved within 1 min with a linear range of 10–500 μM and 10–100 μM for GSH and AA, respectively, and a LOD of 0.476 μM and 0.185 μM. Meanwhile, the successful application of this assay in real samples demonstrated its potential in constructing fluorescent bioassays. In future work, we will make more efforts to distinguish the detection of GSH and AA.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful for the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2023AFB244), Young and Middle-aged Talents Project of Hubei Provincial Department of Education (Q20221712), Wuhan Textile University National Project Cultivation Program, and the Opening project of Key Laboratory of Textile Fiber and Products, Ministry of Education (Fzxw2021023), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFB3805803), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U20A20257), the Special Fund of Taishan Industry Leading Talents Project. We also thank “Hubei International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Intelligent Textile Materials & Application” and “Wuhan Engineering Technology Research Center for Advanced Fibers” for providing partial support for materials processing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: XPS full spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs; FTIR spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs and MoS2 QDs; band gap energy curve of APBA-MoS2 QDs; excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra and normalized fluorescence spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs; size and zeta potential changes of APBA-MoS2 QDs under different storage time; selective fluorescence response map of APBA-MoS2 QDs to MnO4 −; Zeta potential of APBA-MoS2 QDs, MnO4 −, and APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 −; UV–vis absorption spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs added with various concentrations of MnO4 −; UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO4 − and fluorescence excitation spectra of APBA-MoS2 QDs and MoS2 QDs; fluorescence spectra and linear relationship between MnO4 − concentration and fluorescence intensity; fluorescence spectra of response time of GSH and AA; interference study of APBA-MoS2 QDs/MnO4 − system in the sensing of GSH and AA; comparison table of testing performance. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125189.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Chim. Acta 1289 (2024) 342182.
